meta.title: Jurisdiction Risk — Key Seizure & Compulsion Laws
meta.description: Countries with legislation enabling government seizure of cryptographic keys or compelled CA operations.
meta.methodology: Cross-verified against Wikipedia Key Disclosure Law, official legislation sites, EFF, Global Partners Digital, Comparitech, CA/B Forum context.

risk_levels.high: Purpose-built statutory authority across all three compulsion axes (key seizure, compelled issuance, and secrecy).
risk_levels.moderate: Purpose-built statutory authority on one or two axes.
risk_levels.low: No purpose-built compulsion authority. Only general judicial process or constitutionally protected.

axes.key_seizure: Can the government compel disclosure of CA private signing keys?
axes.compelled_issuance: Can the government force a CA to issue a specific certificate?
axes.secrecy: Can the government prohibit the CA from disclosing the compulsion?

axis_values.purpose: Dedicated statutory authority designed for this specific power
axis_values.general: Possible via general judicial process (warrants, subpoenas)
axis_values.none: No authority, or strong constitutional protections prevent it

meta.note: The critical distinction is purpose-built vs general. The combination of compelled_issuance + secrecy is the existential WebPKI threat.
meta.derivation: Risk derived from axes: all three purpose = high, any purpose = moderate, only general or none = low.

jurisdictions.summary: Mandatory cooperation for all organizations (NIL Art. 7/14); full SCA access to systems and keys in critical information infrastructure (Cryptography Law Art. 31).

laws.name: National Intelligence Law (2017)
laws.excerpt: All organizations and citizens shall support, assist, and cooperate with national intelligence efforts in accordance with law.

laws.name: Cryptography Law (2019)
laws.excerpt: SCA and local agencies have complete access to the cryptography system and to the data protected by that system.

laws.name: Cybersecurity Law (2016)
laws.excerpt: Network operators shall provide technical support and assistance to public security organs and national security organs.

jurisdictions.summary: FSB direct access via SORM; Yarovaya Law requires encryption key disclosure without court order.

laws.name: Yarovaya Law (2016)
laws.excerpt: Any online service that uses encrypted data is required to permit the FSB to access encrypted communications, including providing any encryption keys.

laws.name: SORM Regulations (1995+)
laws.excerpt: FSB has access to electronic messages and the keys to decrypt these without judicial authorization.

jurisdictions.summary: RIPA s.49 compels key disclosure with up to 5yr penalty; IPA 2016 Technical Capability Notices; tipping-off ban prevents disclosure to auditors.

laws.name: RIPA 2000, Part III
laws.excerpt: A disclosure requirement is necessary if it is necessary (a) in the interests of national security; (b) for preventing or detecting crime; (c) in the interests of the economic well-being of the UK.

laws.name: Investigatory Powers Act 2016
laws.excerpt: Government can serve Technical Capability Notices requiring operators to remove electronic protection on communications on an ongoing basis.

jurisdictions.summary: TOLA Act TANs/TCNs compel decryption assistance from broad DCP class; 5yr gag order penalty; 10yr for warrant non-compliance.

laws.name: Assistance and Access Act 2018 (TOLA)
laws.excerpt: DCPs include software developers, device manufacturers, and anyone who provides services that facilitate a carriage or electronic service.

jurisdictions.summary: IT Act s.69 executive orders to intermediaries; no judicial oversight; 7yr penalty for noncompliance.

laws.name: Information Technology Act 2000 (amended 2008)
laws.excerpt: The subscriber or intermediary or any person in-charge of the computer resource shall assist the agency to intercept, monitor, or decrypt the information. Failure: imprisonment up to seven years.

jurisdictions.summary: Judicial oversight required; CALEA limits carrier decryption duty; All Writs Act and NSLs (with gag) possible. Lavabit precedent for SSL keys.

laws.name: CALEA (1994)
laws.excerpt: A carrier shall not be responsible for decrypting any communication encrypted by a subscriber or customer, unless the encryption was provided by the carrier.

laws.name: NSLs / All Writs Act
laws.excerpt: Lavabit shut down rather than surrender its master private keys due to the government wanting to spy on Edward Snowden's emails.

jurisdictions.summary: Judicial orders only; 3yr / €270K penalty for refusal.

laws.name: Code of Criminal Procedure
laws.excerpt: A judge may designate a technical expert to obtain plaintext from encrypted data seized during an investigation.

jurisdictions.summary: TICSA requires interception capability; Search and Surveillance Act s.130 compels key access under warrant.

laws.name: TICSA (2013)
laws.excerpt: Network operators must decrypt communications if they have provided the encryption or hold the decryption key.

jurisdictions.summary: RICA s.21 requires provider decryption assistance if key held. Key safeguards struck down by Constitutional Court in 2021; amendments pending.

laws.name: RICA 2002
laws.excerpt: Encrypted communications must be provided in decrypted form if the provider holds the decryption key.

jurisdictions.summary: BTK orders for hosting/access providers; electronic communications law requires interception capability.

laws.name: Law No. 5651 (2007, amended)
laws.excerpt: Hosting and access providers must comply with government orders including providing access to encrypted data.

jurisdictions.summary: E-Commerce Law Art. 43 prohibits encrypting evidence; untested but broadly worded.

laws.name: Law on Electronic Commerce (2019)
laws.excerpt: Prohibits any encryption of evidence in the form of data that could lead to an indictment, or any evidence in an electronic system that relates to an offense.

jurisdictions.summary: Third-party (non-suspect) assistance only; judicial order required. GlobalSign incorporated here.

laws.name: Code of Criminal Procedure
laws.excerpt: A judge may order non-suspect third parties to provide decryption assistance. Suspects protected by self-incrimination principle.

jurisdictions.summary: System admins compelled; suspects exempt under Coercive Measures Act.

laws.name: Coercive Measures Act (2011)
laws.excerpt: System owners and administrators must surrender necessary passwords. Suspects themselves are exempt.

jurisdictions.summary: Strong nemo tenetur; no compulsion for suspects; regulatory readability obligations only in non-criminal contexts.

laws.name: Strafprozessordnung (StPO)
laws.excerpt: Suspects cannot be compelled to hand over any kind of cryptographic key due to the nemo tenetur principle.

jurisdictions.summary: Charter s.11(c) protects against compelled self-incrimination. No statutory key-disclosure law. Strongest Five Eyes protections.

laws.name: Charter of Rights and Freedoms (1982)
laws.excerpt: No legislative power can require individuals to decrypt encrypted communications. The federal government has recognised it has no legislative authority to compel individuals to provide a password.

stats.response_driven_note: 11/15 events have at least one response-quality behavioral tag

compliance_posture._description: The CA's fundamental posture toward compliance. This is the single most important predictor of distrust outcomes — it captures WHY the same type of incident leads to distrust for one CA but not another.
compliance_posture.willful_circumvention: CA knew the rules and built systems or processes to deliberately violate them. Includes backdating, systematic fraud, and active concealment of known violations.
compliance_posture.argumentative_noncompliance: CA understood the rules but argued they shouldn't apply, reinterpreted requirements to justify non-compliance, or positioned compliance obligations as suggestions rather than mandates.
compliance_posture.negligent_noncompliance: CA should have known about and fixed problems but failed to act despite awareness. Knew about deficiencies, had time to fix them, chose not to prioritize.
compliance_posture.demonstrated_incompetence: CA lacked the operational maturity or technical understanding to meet the requirements. Couldn't perform root cause analysis, couldn't maintain audits, didn't understand what was being asked of them.
compliance_posture.accidental: Genuine operational mistake without malice or negligence. The CA's controls failed but the failure wasn't foreseeable or the result of systemic neglect.

reason_tags.infrastructure_compromise: CA infrastructure was breached or compromised, resulting in unauthorized certificate issuance from the CA's own systems.
reason_tags.operational_security_failures: Fundamental security hygiene failures: default credentials, exposed admin interfaces, unpatched systems. The CA didn't know its own systems were vulnerable.
reason_tags.rogue_certificate_issuance: Certificates issued for domains without the holder's authorization, used or usable for MITM interception or impersonation.
reason_tags.baseline_requirements_violations: Systematic violations of CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements: profile errors, encoding issues, missing fields, validity violations.
reason_tags.validation_bypass: Certificates issued without required domain or organization validation, or with insufficient evidence of control.
reason_tags.unauthorized_delegation: Unconstrained subordinate CA certificates issued to third parties without adequate oversight, or those parties misused them.
reason_tags.delayed_or_refused_revocation: Unreasonably delayed revoking misissued certificates, or argued revocation unnecessary — often citing subscriber impact as justification for inaction.
reason_tags.argued_rules_dont_apply: Actively argued requirements should not apply to their situation, reinterpreted standards to justify non-compliance, used CPS language as a shield against BR obligations.
reason_tags.recharacterized_incidents: Changed determination of whether an incident was an incident after filing the report. Retracted misissuance findings or reclassified failures to avoid revocation obligations.
reason_tags.minimized_severity: Consistently downplayed incident significance. 'No security impact' as default posture. Framed systemic problems as isolated events.
reason_tags.lack_of_meaningful_improvement: Despite incidents, warnings, and commitments, failed to demonstrate operational changes. Same patterns repeated. Remediation commitments unmet.
reason_tags.pattern_of_issues: Multiple distinct incidents over months or years indicating systemic organizational problems. The accumulation drove the distrust decision.
reason_tags.active_deception: Deliberately lied to root programs, auditors, or community. Falsified records, backdated certificates, concealed acquisitions, provided knowingly false incident reports.
reason_tags.concealed_breach_or_incident: Discovered a breach or compliance failure and delayed or withheld disclosure rather than reporting promptly.
reason_tags.hidden_corporate_changes: Concealed acquisitions, ownership transfers, or organizational changes that materially affected the CA's operations or independence.
reason_tags.ties_to_adversarial_entities: Corporate or operational ties to organizations involved in surveillance, intelligence, or activities incompatible with public trust.
reason_tags.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Failed to respond to root program inquiries, surveys, or action items. Complete failure to engage.
reason_tags.inadequate_incident_response: Responded but with insufficient depth, transparency, or timeliness. Reports late, incomplete, or missing root cause analysis.
reason_tags.demonstrated_lack_of_understanding: CA responses revealed they did not understand fundamental concepts expected of a trusted CA — e.g., confusing 'root cause' (incident analysis) with 'root certificate', or not understanding what a compliant incident report requires.
reason_tags.audit_deficiencies: Incomplete, qualified, or missing audits required for trust store inclusion.
reason_tags.limited_ecosystem_value: Minimal value to web ecosystem relative to risk of continued inclusion. Unfavorable risk/value ratio.
reason_tags.certificates_used_for_mitm: Fraudulently or improperly issued certificates were confirmed used to intercept encrypted traffic (man-in-the-middle). Distinct from rogue issuance where certs were not confirmed used — this tag means real users' traffic was actually intercepted.

events.summary: CA infrastructure was fully compromised. Attacker issued 531+ fraudulent certificates including *.google.com, used in MITM surveillance of Iranian users. CA concealed the breach for weeks. Declared bankruptcy after distrust.

events.summary: WoSign was distrusted for systematic rule circumvention including backdating SHA-1 certificates, issuing unauthorized certificates, and concealing their acquisition of StartCom. They deliberately built systems to violate browser restrictions and actively deceived the community about their operations.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified primarily from metadata (sparse Bugzilla trail).

evidence.posture_evidence: WoSign systematically backdated SHA-1 certificates to circumvent the January 2016 deprecation deadline, making them appear compliant when they weren't. They built systems to deliberately violate restrictions and actively misled browsers about their secret StartCom acquisition. This is deliberate rule violation with concealment, not mere argumentative non-compliance.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Systematic backdating of SHA-1 certificates after January 2016 deprecation deadline
tag_evidence.rogue_certificate_issuance: Issued certificates for GitHub domain without authorization
tag_evidence.active_deception: Actively attempted to mislead browser community about StartCom acquisition and relationship
tag_evidence.hidden_corporate_changes: Secretly acquired StartCom while both presented themselves as independent CAs
tag_evidence.validation_bypass: Issued certificates for domains without proper validation

articles.title: Distrusting WoSign and StartCom Certificates

events.summary: StartCom was distrusted for concealing its acquisition by WoSign while maintaining false appearance of independence. Post-distrust bugs show continued attempts to circumvent restrictions through proxy issuance and certificate manipulation, demonstrating willful violation of trust boundaries.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: StartCom deliberately built systems to violate trust requirements by concealing its acquisition by WoSign and maintaining the appearance of independence while sharing infrastructure and management. Post-distrust, they systematically attempted to circumvent the distrust through proxy issuance (Bug 1350615) and 'un-revoking' intermediate certificates (Bug 1369342), demonstrating ongoing intentional violation of trust boundaries.

tag_evidence.active_deception: StartCom 'maintained the appearance of being an independent CA while sharing WoSign's infrastructure and management' - deliberately presented false independence
tag_evidence.hidden_corporate_changes: StartCom 'had replaced infrastructure, staff, policies, and issuance systems with WoSign's' following an undisclosed acquisition
tag_evidence.rogue_certificate_issuance: Bug 1369359: 'mis-issuance of certs with unvalidated domain names and bogus field values'
tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1386894: 'Non-BR-Compliant Certificate Issuance' and multiple CAA violations in Bugs 1409760, 1409859
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 8 distinct bugs from 2017-2019 showing systematic ongoing violations after distrust
tag_evidence.validation_bypass: Bug 1369359 indicates certificates issued 'with unvalidated domain names'

events.summary: Google documented years of misissuance incidents including test certificates, non-audited intermediates, and BR violations. DigiCert acquired Symantec's CA business and managed the transition. Gradual distrust over 12+ months with subscriber migration.

events.posture_evidence: Third-party RA program was knowingly structured to bypass validation controls. Repeatedly minimized severity and argued rules should not apply as written. Willful misissuance in training infrastructure. Pattern of argumentative engagement with root programs eroded confidence.

events.summary: TurkTrust was distrusted following a pattern of compliance failures including issuing non-audited, non-constrained intermediate certificates and failing to respond to root program surveys. The distrust was triggered by the 2012 incident where their operational mistake led to a fraudulent Google certificate, followed by ongoing compliance deficiencies.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified primarily from metadata (sparse Bugzilla trail).

evidence.posture_evidence: TurkTrust understood their obligations but failed to prioritize compliance despite having time to fix known deficiencies. Bug 1367842 shows they had non-audited, non-technically-constrained intermediates for years without addressing this, and Bug 1439127 shows they simply didn't respond to required surveys. Their 2018 response mentions 'strategic reorganization' as an excuse, indicating they knew about obligations but chose not to prioritize them.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1367842: 'Non-audited, non-technically-constrained intermediate certs' - systematic BR violations regarding intermediate certificate requirements
tag_evidence.unauthorized_delegation: Bug 1367842: issuing 'non-technically-constrained intermediate certs' without proper oversight or constraints
tag_evidence.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Bug 1439127: 'Failure to respond to January 2018 survey' - complete failure to engage with required root program communications
tag_evidence.audit_deficiencies: Bug 1367842: 'Non-audited' intermediate certificates - missing required audits for trust store inclusion
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: Two separate bugs spanning May 2017 to February 2018 showing ongoing compliance failures
tag_evidence.rogue_certificate_issuance: Reference context: intermediate certificate was 'subsequently used to issue a fraudulent certificate for *.google.com' - unauthorized certificate for major domain

articles.title: Turkish CA enabled phishers to spoof Google
Enhancing digital certificate security

milestones.detail: Two intermediate CA certs accidentally issued to customers

Google, Mozilla, Microsoft revoke trust in misissued intermediates

events.summary: Certinomis was distrusted in 2019 following a sustained pattern of 20 documented incidents over 2+ years showing systematic BR violations including invalid DNS names, improper certificate profiles, and OCSP failures. Despite being aware of these issues through bug reports, the CA failed to implement effective systemic remediation, with the same violation categories recurring throughout the period.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: Certinomis was aware of problems through repeated bug reports from 2017-2019 but failed to implement effective systemic fixes. They responded to individual incidents but the 20 bugs show the same patterns recurring (invalid DNS names, BR violations, OCSP failures), demonstrating they knew about deficiencies but didn't prioritize comprehensive remediation.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Multiple bugs show systematic BR violations: Bug 1390978 (Non-BR-Compliant Certificate Issuance), Bug 1524449 (validity period >825 days), Bug 1524451 (invalid CDP extension), Bug 1503128 (email address in DNS SAN)
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 20 distinct bugs over 2+ years (2017-2019) showing repeated categories of violations: invalid DNS names (Bugs 1524094, 1539531, 1551357), test certificates (Bugs 1496088, 1524112, 1524448), OCSP issues (Bugs 1425998, 1544933, 1551390)
tag_evidence.lack_of_meaningful_improvement: Same violation types recurring throughout the period - invalid DNS names appearing in bugs from 2019-01-30 (Bug 1524094) through 2019-05-13 (Bug 1551357), showing systemic problems weren't fixed
tag_evidence.inadequate_incident_response: Bug 1386891 shows incomplete response about cross-signing disclosure, with partial response cut off mid-sentence, and Bug 1390978 incident report appears incomplete based on cached context
tag_evidence.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Bug 1439126 explicitly documents 'Failure to respond to January 2018 survey'

articles.title: Mozilla distrusts Certinomis issued certificates

milestones.detail: First Bugzilla bug (cross-signing StartCom intermediates)

events.summary: PROCERT was a Venezuelan CA that demonstrated incompetence through non-BR-compliant issuance and complete non-responsiveness to Mozilla's action items. The sparse 2017 bug trail with no CA engagement suggests an organization that lacked the operational capacity to participate meaningfully in the WebPKI ecosystem.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified primarily from metadata (sparse Bugzilla trail).

evidence.posture_evidence: The sparse Bugzilla trail with only 2 bugs from 2017 and complete absence of CA responses indicates PROCERT lacked the operational maturity to engage with root program requirements. The progression from 'Non-BR-Compliant Certificate Issuance' to unresolved 'Action Items' with no CA engagement suggests they didn't understand what was being asked of them rather than choosing not to comply.

tag_evidence.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Complete absence of CA responses in both Bug 1391058 and Bug 1405862 despite action items being raised
tag_evidence.demonstrated_lack_of_understanding: Failure to respond to Bug 1405862 'Action Items' suggests fundamental disconnect with root program expectations and processes
tag_evidence.limited_ecosystem_value: Venezuelan CA with minimal engagement and sparse bug history indicates limited value to global web ecosystem

articles.title: Mozilla distrusts PROCERT: local CAs have global impact

milestones.detail: First Bugzilla bug (non-BR-compliant issuance)

events.summary: Camerfirma was distrusted after years of systematic Baseline Requirements violations, delayed revocations, and unauthorized subordinate CA issuance. Despite repeated incidents and Mozilla oversight, the CA failed to implement effective controls, with the same types of violations recurring over multiple years.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: Camerfirma repeatedly acknowledged compliance violations and made commitments to fix them, but failed to implement effective systemic controls. Bug 1609828 shows they understood Mozilla Policy requirements but made a 'decision not to revoke' non-compliant certificates. Bug 1647099 documents 'delayed revocations related to Invalid authorityKeyIdentifier - recurrent incident', showing they knew about the problem but failed to prevent recurrence. This distinguishes it from incompetence (they understood the requirements) and from willful circumvention (they weren't systematically building processes to violate rules).

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Multiple bugs document systematic BR violations: Bug 1586860 'Invalid authorityKeyIdentifier, violating Mozilla Policy and RFC 5280', Bug 1600114 'EV Certificates issued with wrong Business Category', Bug 1667430 'Invalid stateOrProvinceName field'
tag_evidence.delayed_or_refused_revocation: Bug 1609828 'Decision not to revoke certificates with authorityKeyIdentifier that violates Mozilla Policy', Bug 1647099 'Delayed revocations related to Invalid authorityKeyIdentifier', Bug 1652603 'Failure to revoke within 7 days: OCSP EKU issue'
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 46 bugs spanning 2017-2021 showing repeated compliance failures across multiple areas: certificate profiles, validation, revocation, subordinate CA management, and audit compliance
tag_evidence.lack_of_meaningful_improvement: Bug 1623384 and 1623389 both titled 'Invalid authorityKeyIdentifier - recurrent incident' showing same issues repeating despite previous incidents and commitments to fix
tag_evidence.unauthorized_delegation: Bug 1672029 'Failure to abide by Section 8 of Mozilla Policy: Unauthorized, improperly disclosed Subordinate CA', Bug 1672562 'Incorrect disclosure of Intesa Sanpaolo sub-CA', Bug 1688215 'CP/CPS of Intesa Sanpaolo Sub-CA is Non-Compliant'
tag_evidence.audit_deficiencies: Bug 1583470 'audit gap', Bug 1704140 'Govern d'Andorra Audit Delay' showing failure to maintain required audit coverage
tag_evidence.inadequate_incident_response: CA responses show defensive posture and insufficient depth - for example, Bug 1704140 response shows they only realized audit delay on April 7th despite being told March 30th, indicating poor incident management

events.summary: TrustCor was distrusted in 2022 primarily due to discovered corporate ties to surveillance entities rather than the technical compliance issues shown in these 2019 Bugzilla reports. While they had routine compliance problems and delayed revocations, the fundamental issue was organizational trust incompatibility.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: TrustCor understood the requirements but consistently failed to prioritize fixing known compliance issues. Bug 1599571 shows they delayed revoking subordinate CA certificates beyond the required 7-day window, and their response shows they knew the requirements but chose to extend timelines for operational convenience. This distinguishes it from demonstrated incompetence because they understood what was required.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1532399 (insufficient serial number entropy), Bug 1568356 (incorrect CA-Issuers URI)
tag_evidence.delayed_or_refused_revocation: Bug 1599571 shows failure to revoke CA certificates within 7 days, with CA response indicating they needed 'additional time to assess'
tag_evidence.audit_deficiencies: Bug 1599503 shows TLS-capable intermediate CAs were not mentioned in WTBR audit reports, and Bug 1579284 indicates non-audited intermediate certificates
tag_evidence.ties_to_adversarial_entities: Reference context indicates corporate connections to companies linked to US intelligence surveillance operations (Packet Forensics)
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 5 distinct compliance bugs spanning from 2019-2019, showing systematic operational problems

events.summary: Visa demonstrated a pattern of BR violations and audit deficiencies from 2017-2020 while completely failing to engage with root programs or respond to identified issues. The CA showed no meaningful participation in the WebPKI ecosystem it was supposed to serve.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified primarily from metadata (sparse Bugzilla trail).

evidence.posture_evidence: Visa knew about identified compliance problems (Bug 1391087 non-BR-compliant issuance, Bug 1398261 OCSP issues, Bug 1485851 qualified audits) but completely failed to engage with root programs or demonstrate any remediation efforts over a 3+ year period. This goes beyond incompetence into willful neglect of known obligations.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1391087 'Non-BR-Compliant Certificate Issuance' and Bug 1398261 'Non-BR-Compliant OCSP Responders'
tag_evidence.audit_deficiencies: Bug 1485851 'Qualified Audit Statements' indicating incomplete or deficient security audits
tag_evidence.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Context notes 'Visa completely failed to participate in root program policy discussions or respond to identified issues'
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: Four distinct bugs spanning 2017-2020 showing ongoing compliance failures without resolution
tag_evidence.limited_ecosystem_value: Context describes 'no meaningful engagement with the ecosystem it was supposed to participate in'

events.summary: E-Tugra was distrusted after security researcher Ian Carroll discovered severe operational security failures including default passwords and exposed administrative interfaces. The CA had a 4-year pattern of baseline requirements violations but the security research findings triggered immediate distrust action.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: E-Tugra had default passwords on production systems, publicly accessible administrative interfaces, and exposed internal tools that they were unaware of until a security researcher discovered them. Their response revealed fundamental misunderstandings (bad English explanations, confusion about their own architecture), and they couldn't perform adequate incident analysis despite multiple attempts. This shows lack of operational maturity rather than willful violation.

tag_evidence.operational_security_failures: Security researcher Ian Carroll found default passwords on production systems, publicly accessible administrative interfaces, and exposed internal tools
tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1449371 (validity > 825 days), Bug 1462797 (invalid DER/RFC 5280), Bug 1542302 (insufficient serial entropy), Bug 1582601 (string length violations), Bug 1687139 (commonName not in SAN)
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 14 bugs spanning 2018-2022 showing systematic problems: survey non-response (Bugs 1439128, 1772414), encoding issues, revocation delays, audit problems
tag_evidence.inadequate_incident_response: Root programs repeatedly noted sparse penetration test reports lacking actionable detail (Comment by cclements@google.com and bwilson@mozilla.com requesting more substantive reports)
tag_evidence.demonstrated_lack_of_understanding: CA responses revealed confusion about their own architecture, admitted 'bad English' explanations, and needed multiple clarification rounds about basic operational questions
tag_evidence.non_responsive_to_root_programs: Bug 1439128 (failure to respond to January 2018 survey), Bug 1772414 (failure to respond to May 2022 survey)
tag_evidence.limited_ecosystem_value: Small regional CA with demonstrated security incompetence and persistent compliance issues over 4+ years

events.summary: Ecommerce Monitoring demonstrated fundamental operational incompetence through a sustained pattern of baseline requirements violations and delayed revocations spanning 2021-2024. Most notably, when asked for root cause analysis of incidents, they confused the investigative concept with their root certificate, revealing they didn't understand basic CA operational requirements.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: The CA fundamentally misunderstood basic incident response concepts, confusing 'root cause analysis' (investigation methodology) with 'root certificate' when asked to analyze incidents. This demonstrates lack of operational maturity rather than willful violation or negligence - they literally didn't understand what was being asked of them.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Bug 1716123 (CN domain not in SAN), Bug 1883711 (precertificate validity mismatch), Bug 1888371 (CRLs with mismatched issuer)
tag_evidence.delayed_or_refused_revocation: Bug 1862004 specifically about delayed revocation, requiring separate incident report per Mozilla requirements
tag_evidence.inadequate_incident_response: Bug 1893546 (failure to follow incident report requirements) and root program comments noting responses 'fallen short of expectations'
tag_evidence.demonstrated_lack_of_understanding: Reference context notes CA confused 'root cause analysis' with 'root certificate' when asked for incident investigation
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 9 bugs spanning 2021-2024 showing systematic operational problems across multiple technical areas
tag_evidence.lack_of_meaningful_improvement: Sustained issues over 3+ years with root program comments noting 'sustained and willing non-compliance' despite multiple incidents

events.summary: Entrust demonstrated a multi-year pattern of baseline requirements violations, delayed revocations, and argumentative responses that prioritized justifying non-compliance over fixing issues. Chrome explicitly noted Entrust's pattern of arguing that established practices should not apply to their circumstances rather than taking responsibility for mistakes.
events.classification_tier_note: Classified from Bugzilla evidence + cached metadata. Both sources available.

evidence.posture_evidence: Entrust understood the requirements but consistently argued they shouldn't apply to their specific circumstances. Chrome Root Program explicitly noted Entrust 'continues to disregard the commitments made in its own policy documents...instead of taking responsibility for its mistakes and adhering to established practices, Entrust instead appears to continue a pattern that prioritizes arguing the agreed-upon practices should not apply.' This is argumentative noncompliance rather than willful circumvention because they didn't build systems to violate rules, but rather reinterpreted requirements to justify non-compliance.

tag_evidence.baseline_requirements_violations: Multiple bugs show systematic BR violations: Bug 1888714 (EV Certificate missing Issuer's EV Policy OID), Bug 1883843 (EV TLS Certificate cPSuri missing), Bug 1889217 (CRL non-conformance with TLS BRs)
tag_evidence.delayed_or_refused_revocation: Bug 1851710 (Delay beyond 5 days in revoking misissued certificates), Bug 1886532 (Delayed revocation of EV TLS certificates with missing cPSuri), Bug 1887705 (Delayed revocation of clientAuth TLS Certificates without serverAuth EKU)
tag_evidence.argued_rules_dont_apply: Chrome noted 'Entrust continues to disregard the commitments made in its own policy documents...instead of taking responsibility for its mistakes and adhering to established practices, Entrust instead appears to continue a pattern that prioritizes arguing the agreed-upon practices should not apply'
tag_evidence.recharacterized_incidents: Chrome's reference context indicates Entrust 'recharacterized misissuance as non-incidents after filing incident reports'
tag_evidence.minimized_severity: Chrome's reference context indicates Entrust 'used subscriber impact as justification for not revoking misissued certificates'
tag_evidence.lack_of_meaningful_improvement: Chrome stated 'Over the past several years, publicly disclosed incident reports highlighted a pattern of concerning behaviors by Entrust that fall short of the above expectations, and has eroded confidence in their competence, reliability, and integrity'
tag_evidence.pattern_of_issues: 100+ bugs spanning 2022-2024 showing systematic organizational problems across multiple areas: OCSP, CRLs, certificate profiles, revocation delays, incident response
tag_evidence.inadequate_incident_response: Bug 1890123 (Failed to provide a preliminary incident report according to TLS BR 4.9.5), Bug 1885754 (CPR was not responded to in 24 hours)

meta.note_technical: technical_comments counts LLM-classified substantive comments (cert/CRL analysis, specific BR citations, investigative questions). Values may exceed bugs_engaged since multiple technical comments per bug are counted.
meta.note_baseline: All CABF CA members included as org rows, even those with zero engagement. cabf_member=true indicates formal membership. Absence of engagement by a member is as meaningful as presence.
